title: Gravity Echoes from Supermassive Black Hole Binaries
comment: 13 pages, 6 tables, 6 figures
summary: Pulsar timing arrays record gravitational waves from supermassive black hole binaries at two spacetime points: an Earth term, measured when the wave passes the Earth, and a pulsar term, measured when the wave passed each pulsar at an earlier epoch. We show that a future $μ$Hz-band detection of a nearby massive binary by a mission such as $μ$Ares would turn PTA pulsar terms into targeted probes of binary evolution. In analogy with supernova light echoes, each pulsar term acts as a gravity echo: a dated snapshot of the binary at an earlier stage of its inspiral. Together, the $μ$Hz Earth-term measurement and the nHz pulsar-term echoes provide a temporal baseline that neither detector could access alone. For a fiducial equal-mass binary with total mass $10^9\,M_\odot$ at 80~Mpc, we find a combined pulsar timing array echo signal-to-noise ratio of 33, with up to 24 pulsars individually resolving the signal among pulsars with 50-year baselines. The angular dependence of the single-pulsar echo sensitivity alone enables independent sky localization of the source to $\sim$10--100~deg$^2$, and the resolved pulsar-term frequencies directly measure the binary inspiral rate hundreds to thousands of years ago. With sufficient pulsar distance precision, a small set of anchor pulsars could additionally phase-connect the array and trace the post-Newtonian evolution coherently over kpc baselines. The source population required for gravity echoes is drawn from the same massive-end census responsible for the observed nanoHertz stochastic background.

AI.tldr: Pulsar timing arrays can use μHz-band detections of massive binaries to turn pulsar terms into "gravity echoes" that probe binary evolution historically, creating temporal baselines spanning hundreds to thousands of years.
AI.motivation: PTAs currently detect gravitational waves at two spacetime points (Earth term and pulsar term), but pulsar terms have not been fully utilized as historical probes of binary evolution. The authors aim to show how future μHz missions can transform these pulsar terms into targeted probes of binary inspiral history.
AI.method: The authors analyze a fiducial equal-mass binary with total mass 10^9 M⊙ at 80 Mpc. They compute combined PTA echo SNR, assess single-pulsar angular resolution, measure resolved pulsar-term frequencies to infer historical inspiral rates, and propose phase-connecting the array using anchor pulsars with precise distance measurements.
AI.result: Combined PTA echo SNR of 33 is achievable with up to 24 pulsars resolving the signal among 50-year baselines. Single-pulsar echo sensitivity enables sky localization to ~10-100 deg². Resolved pulsar-term frequencies directly measure inspiral rates hundreds to thousands of years ago.
AI.conclusion: Gravity echoes from μHz+PTA synergy enable temporal baselines neither detector could access alone, with source populations drawn from the same massive-end census responsible for the nHz stochastic background.

title: Archival Multiband Gravitational-Wave Signals from Massive Black Hole Binary Mergers
comment: 20 pages, 5 figures
summary: While massive black hole binaries (MBHBs) merge at gravitational-wave frequencies above the pulsar timing array (PTA) sensitivity band, we show that they leave orphaned low-frequency contributions in the PTA pulsar term. Due to the light-propagation time between each pulsar in the array and Earth, the pulsar term acts as a time-delayed probe of a chirping merger with a specific frequency response determined by the direction of origin and intrinsic properties of the MBHB. We provide a detailed consideration of how such a multiband signal would manifest in a full PTA, demonstrate an approach to stack these orphaned pulsar terms across the array, and discuss prospects for an archival, multiband search in conjunction with MBHB mergers observed in astrometric data or spaceborne interferometers like LISA.

AI.tldr: Massive black hole binaries (MBHBs) merging at frequencies above the PTA band leave orphaned low-frequency signals in the pulsar term due to light-travel time delays, enabling multiband gravitational-wave searches across PTAs, astrometry, and spaceborne interferometers like LISA.
AI.motivation: MBHBs merge at gravitational-wave frequencies too high for pulsar timing arrays (PTAs) to detect directly, but their pulsar terms — delayed signals from the binary's earlier chirp — may still be observable at low frequencies, motivating an archival multiband search strategy.
AI.method: The paper analyzes how the pulsar term's frequency response depends on the MBHB's sky location and intrinsic properties, and presents a stacking approach to combine orphaned pulsar-term signals across the full PTA array for enhanced detectability.
AI.result: Orphaned pulsar terms from high-frequency MBHB mergers can be detected in PTAs as low-frequency signals, with a characteristic frequency response shaped by the source's geometry and evolution.
AI.conclusion: Such orphaned signals enable a multiband gravitational-wave search strategy that connects PTA observations with astrometric surveys and spaceborne detectors like LISA, expanding the observational reach for MBHB mergers.

title: An Analytic Threshold for LESA-Driven Negative ELN Flux Directions in Core-Collapse Supernovae: Derivation and Population Census
summary: In core-collapse supernovae (CCSNe), deleptonization normally favors $ν_e$ over $\barν_e$ emission. However, lepton-number emission self-sustained asymmetry (LESA) can make the energy-integrated emitted lepton-number flux negative along some directions. We derive a simple diagnostic for this transition and test it in 33 independent 3D CCSN simulations: 25 Princeton/Fornax models ($8.1$--$100\,M_\odot$) and 8 Garching models, including non-, slow-, and fast-rotating $15\,M_\odot$ cases. Of 23 non-black-hole-forming Princeton models, 22 cross the threshold, with median onset $t_c=225\,\mathrm{ms}$, IQR $162$--$264\,\mathrm{ms}$, and cross-model scatter $\mathrm{CV}=18.6\%$. Full-sky flux-sign searches show that the threshold identifies the anti-LESA-pole transition, distinguishing the global LESA-driven crossing from early localized turbulent crossings. The fast-rotating Garching $15\,M_\odot$ model, where rapid rotation suppresses the LESA dipole, is correctly classified as a non-crosser without using any rotation parameter. Both black-hole-forming Princeton models cross near $250\,\mathrm{ms}$ post-bounce and remain above threshold for $1807$ and $2463\,\mathrm{ms}$ before collapse. Thus, in the next nearby CCSN, the emitted $\barν_e$ energy flux may exceed the $ν_e$ flux along some lines of sight. Such directions may also correlate with sustained fast flavor instability, although testing this requires local phase-space distributions or dedicated linear stability analysis. The relevant quantity here is the energy-integrated emitted flux field, i.e. a luminosity difference per steradian, not a neutrino number flux.

AI.motivation: To derive and validate a simple diagnostic for when the energy-integrated emitted lepton-number flux becomes negative (anti-LESA-pole transition) in core-collapse supernovae, and to systematically test this diagnostic across a large set of independent 3D simulations to understand its universality and connection to neutrino emission asymmetries.
AI.method: A diagnostic threshold for LESA transition is derived. It is tested on 33 independent 3D CCSN simulations: 25 Princeton/Fornax models (8.1–100 $M_\odot$) and 8 Garching models (including non-, slow-, and fast-rotating 15 $M_\odot$ cases). Full-sky flux-sign searches are used to distinguish global LESA-driven crossings from early localized turbulent crossings.
AI.result: 22 out of 23 non-black-hole-forming Princeton models cross the threshold (median onset 225 ms, IQR 162–264 ms, CV=18.6%). The fast-rotating Garching model is correctly classified as non-crosser. Both black-hole-forming models cross near 250 ms and remain above threshold for ~1807 and 2463 ms before collapse.
AI.conclusion: In the next nearby CCSN, the emitted $\bar{\nu}_e$ energy flux may exceed $\nu_e$ flux along some lines of sight. Such directions may correlate with sustained fast flavor instability. The relevant quantity is the energy-integrated emitted flux field, i.e., luminosity difference per steradian, not neutrino number flux.

title: Revisiting radio synchrotron diagnostics in star-forming galaxies
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures. Submitted to MNRAS
summary: Radio continuum observations are widely used to study cosmic ray (CR) electron populations and transport processes in star-forming galaxies, but their interpretation relies on several simplifying assumptions. Here, we revisit three common assumptions: that some vertical radio profiles can be explained by CR advection alone, that radio spectra directly trace the galaxy-wide CR electron spectrum, and that bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses are negligible for radio-emitting electrons. We model radio emission using time-dependent CR electron evolution in a magnetohydrodynamical simulation of an isolated Milky Way-mass galaxy. CR electron spectra are evolved self-consistently along Lagrangian tracer particles with the CREST framework, including injection at supernova remnants, advection with the gas, and spatially and temporally varying radiative losses. We compare these results to commonly adopted steady-state models. We find that advection-only transport in self-consistently driven galactic winds fails to reproduce the extended vertical radio intensity profiles observed in edge-on galaxies, despite reproducing the observed steepening of spectral indices with height. This is because slowly accelerating winds keep electrons in strong cooling environments for too long. Matching observed radio haloes with advection alone requires unrealistically high midplane wind velocities, implying that additional transport or re-acceleration processes are required. Although galaxy-integrated CR electron spectra at radio-emitting energies are similar across models, the resulting synchrotron spectra differ systematically because radio emission is biased toward young electrons in dense, strongly magnetised regions. Finally, we show that bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses significantly shape radio spectra even when their loss rate is subdominant and therefore cannot be neglected.

AI.tldr: This paper uses MHD simulations with self-consistent CR electron modeling to test three common assumptions in radio continuum observations of galaxies: that vertical radio profiles can be explained by CR advection alone, that radio spectra directly trace the galaxy-wide CR electron spectrum, and that bremsstrahlung/Coulomb losses are negligible. All three assumptions are found to be invalid.
AI.motivation: Radio continuum observations of star-forming galaxies are widely used to study CR electron populations and transport, but their interpretation relies on several simplifying assumptions that have not been rigorously tested.
AI.method: Time-dependent CR electron evolution modeled in a magnetohydrodynamical simulation of an isolated Milky Way-mass galaxy using the CREST framework, with self-consistent injection at supernova remnants, advection with gas, and spatially/temporally varying radiative losses along Lagrangian tracer particles.
AI.result: (1) Advection-only transport in self-consistent galactic winds cannot reproduce observed vertical radio intensity profiles, requiring unrealistic wind velocities; (2) Synchrotron spectra differ systematically from galaxy-integrated CR electron spectra due to emission bias toward young electrons in dense, magnetized regions; (3) Bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses significantly shape radio spectra even when subdominant.
AI.conclusion: All three common assumptions are invalid: additional transport/re-acceleration is needed, radio spectra do not directly trace the galaxy-wide CR electron spectrum, and bremsstrahlung/Coulomb losses cannot be neglected.

title: Geometry, Not Calorimetry, Drives the Radio/Infrared/Gamma-Ray Correlation
comment: 17 pages, 9 figures. ApJ submitted
summary: We investigate whether the observed radio-infrared-$γ$-ray correlation in star-forming galaxies is a geometric effect rather than a signature of local cosmic-ray (CR) calorimetry. Using the GALPROP framework, we generate synthetic observations for external viewers from a grid of 3D Milky Way models with varied CR source, gas, interstellar radiation, and magnetic field distributions, all normalised to reproduce local CR data. We find that a tight, quasi-linear correlation arises naturally from line-of-sight integration through the extended, radially-structured disc, even when local calorimetry is absent. The correlation's properties depend strongly on viewing geometry, preserving its form under moderate inclination but breaking down in edge-on views where galactic components are stratified. We conclude that the correlation is primarily an emergent property of geometric projection, not local physics. This implies that its scatter is likely not random noise but a diagnostic of underlying galactic structure and viewing angle.

AI.tldr: The radio-infrared-γ-ray correlation in star-forming galaxies arises primarily from geometric line-of-sight integration through the extended disc structure rather than from local cosmic-ray calorimetry.
AI.motivation: To test whether the observed multi-wavelength correlation in star-forming galaxies reflects local physical processes (CR calorimetry) or is instead an emergent geometric effect from projection.
AI.method: Used the GALPROP framework to generate synthetic observations from a grid of 3D Milky Way models with varied distributions of CR sources, gas, interstellar radiation, and magnetic fields, all normalized to local CR data.
AI.result: A tight, quasi-linear correlation emerges naturally from line-of-sight integration through the extended disc even without local calorimetry. Correlation properties depend on viewing geometry: preserved under moderate inclination but breaks down in edge-on views.
AI.conclusion: The correlation is an emergent property of geometric projection, not local physics; its scatter may serve as a diagnostic of galactic structure and viewing angle rather than random noise.

title: Bayesian Inference of Dense-Matter Equations of State from Small-Radius Compact Stars with Twin-Star Scenarios
comment: 24 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables,
summary: We investigate dense-matter equations of state (EOSs) within a Bayesian framework, with particular emphasis on whether recent small-radius compact-star candidates can be accommodated in a twin-star scenario. For the hadronic sector, we adopt a meta-modeling EOS constrained by the NICER mass--radius measurements of PSR J0030$+$0451, PSR J0437$-$4715, PSR J0614$-$3329, and the massive pulsar PSR J0740$+$6620. The hadronic inference indicates that PSR J0614$-$3329 favors a somewhat softer EOS than the other two \(\sim1.4\,M_\odot\) pulsars, while the \(\sim2\,M_\odot\) constraint prevents the EOS from becoming too soft. We then introduce a strong first-order phase transition through a constant-speed-of-sound quark-matter segment. Using HESS J1731$-$347 and XTE J1814$-$338 to constrain the phase-transition parameters, we find a preferred transition density of \(n_\mathrm{t}\sim2.7\text{--}2.8\,n_0\), a sizable energy-density jump of \(600\text{--}700\) MeV, and a relatively large post-transition sound speed of \(c_s^2/c^2\sim0.85\). Such a phase transition generates a disconnected hybrid branch with radii of about \(6\text{--}7\) km at masses around \(1.2\text{--}1.4\,M_\odot\), and strongly suppresses the dimensionless tidal deformability relative to the purely hadronic branch. This pronounced change in tidal deformability is a characteristic signature of the twin-star mechanism and may provide an important observational tool for identifying phase transitions in neutron-star matter in future multimessenger measurements. These results show that small-radius compact stars can provide direct constraints on both the strength of a first-order phase transition and the stiffness of the post-transition phase in dense matter.

AI.tldr: The paper uses Bayesian inference to study dense-matter equations of state, finding that a strong first-order phase transition to quark matter (at ~2.7-2.8 n₀ with large energy jump and high sound speed) can produce twin-star configurations with radii of 6-7 km, consistent with small-radius compact-star candidates like HESS J1731-347 and XTE J1814-338. The resulting suppression of tidal deformability provides a key observational signature for multimessenger detection of phase transitions.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether small-radius compact-star candidates can be explained via a twin-star scenario involving a first-order phase transition, and to characterize how such transitions affect observable neutron-star properties.
AI.method: Bayesian framework combining hadronic meta-modeling EOS (constrained by NICER mass-radius measurements of PSR J0030+0451, J0437-4715, J0614-3329, and massive pulsar J0740+6620) with a constant-speed-of-sound quark-matter segment for the phase transition, using HESS J1731-347 and XTE J1814-338 to constrain transition parameters.
AI.result: Preferred transition density ~2.7-2.8 n₀, energy-density jump 600-700 MeV, post-transition sound speed ~0.85 c²; the phase transition creates a disconnected hybrid branch with radii ~6-7 km at ~1.2-1.4 M⊙ and strongly suppresses tidal deformability relative to the hadronic branch.
AI.conclusion: Small-radius compact stars can directly constrain the strength of first-order phase transitions and post-transition stiffness; the characteristic tidal deformability suppression is a key signature for identifying phase transitions in future multimessenger observations.

title: Characterizing the GD-1 Stream with DESI DR2 Data: Thin Stream and Hot Cocoon
comment: 29 pages, 15 figures, 8 tables, submitted to ApJ, comments are welcome, associated data for all Tables is available on zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19258456) including a jupyter notebook tutorial, see paper for more details
summary: GD-1 is among the longest, coldest stellar streams in the Milky Way, making it an ideal target for probing dark matter substructure through dynamical heating. We present a catalog of 608 spectroscopically confirmed GD-1 members from the first three years of Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) observations. This constitutes the largest homogeneous spectroscopic sample of GD-1, doubling the number of members previously available only through heterogeneous compilations combining multiple surveys with different systematics. Using these data, we derive updated stream tracks in sky position, proper motion, and radial velocity that extend over $100^\circ$ of the stream. We apply a Gaussian mixture model to decompose the stream into a dynamically cold thin component ($σ_V = 2.49\pm 0.28$ km s$^{-1}$, width $= 0.23\pm0.01^\circ$) and a kinematically hot cocoon ($σ_V = 6.13\pm0.75$ km s$^{-1}$, width $= 2.18\pm0.17^\circ$). The cocoon contains $\sim30\%$ of members and its velocity dispersion is consistent with $\sim11$ Gyr of heating by cold dark matter subhalos. We also detect a large proper motion dispersion ($41.36\pm4.98$ km s$^{-1}$) along the stream direction in the cocoon component. This feature indicates a significant line-of-sight distance spread in the cocoon, and its origin will be further explored in a forthcoming paper. These measurements demonstrate the power of DESI spectroscopy for characterizing the multi-component phase-space structure of stellar streams and constraining small-scale dark matter substructure.

AI.tldr: Using 608 spectroscopically confirmed GD-1 stream members from DESI (doubling available samples), we derive updated stream tracks over 100° and decompose the stream into a cold thin component (σ_V=2.49 km/s, width=0.23°) and a hot cocoon (σ_V=6.13 km/s, width=2.18°, ~30% of members), with the cocoon's velocity dispersion consistent with ~11 Gyr of heating by cold dark matter subhalos. A large proper motion dispersion (41.36 km/s) along the stream in the cocoon suggests significant line-of-sight distance spread.
AI.motivation: GD-1 is one of the longest, coldest stellar streams, ideal for probing dark matter substructure via dynamical heating.
AI.method: DESI spectroscopic observations over three years; Gaussian mixture model to decompose thin and cocoon components.
AI.result: 608 members (largest homogeneous sample); updated tracks over 100°; cold component σ_V=2.49±0.28 km/s, width=0.23±0.01°; cocoon σ_V=6.13±0.75 km/s, width=2.18±0.17° with ~30% of members; proper motion dispersion 41.36±4.98 km/s along stream in cocoon.
AI.conclusion: Cocoon velocity dispersion consistent with ~11 Gyr heating by cold dark matter subhalos; demonstrates DESI's power for constraining small-scale dark matter substructure via multi-component phase-space analysis of stellar streams.

title: Light, heavy, primordial: exploring the diversity of black hole seeding and growth mechanisms in the JWST era
comment: Submitted to OJA; comments welcome
summary: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) has revealed a puzzling population of massive black holes in the first billion years, many of which are over-massive compared to their hosts (obese black holes), and reside in metal-poor hosts, posing a challenge for theoretical models at these early epochs. In this work, we compare the observational properties of astrophysically-seeded black holes using the DELPHI semi-analytic model and cosmologically-seeded primordial black holes (PBHs) using the PHANES analytic model. We explore the growth of light ($\sim 100 M_\odot$) and heavy ($\sim 10^{3-5}M_\odot$) seeds through mergers and accretion (both Eddington-limited and at super-Eddington rates) in the astrophysical scenario; PBHs (seeded between $10^{0.5-6}M_\odot$) only grow through accretion at sub-Eddington rates. Comparing to observables at $z \sim 5-10$, the only model that can be ruled out is the one where we allow Eddington-limited accretion onto light seeds. The observed high values of the black hole mass-stellar mass relation ($0.3-1$) can be reproduced by both PBHs and heavy seeds accreting at super-Eddington rates. However, only the PBH and Eddington-limited heavy seeding models can simultaneously reproduce the observed black hole masses (${\rm M_{bh}}$), stellar masses ($M_*$), and extremely low host metallicities ($Z \leq 0.01 Z_\odot$) inferred at $z \sim 7-10$. Crucially, we find PBHs show decrease in the black hole mass-stellar mass ratio with increasing halo mass at all redshifts, contrary to any astrophysical black hole model. Selecting systems at $z \sim 7$ with ${\rm M_{bh}}/M_* > 0.1$ and bolometric luminosities $\sim 10^{44-46} {\rm erg~s^{-1}}$ that show a negative black hole to stellar mass ratio and reside in $10^{9-11}M_\odot$ halos offer a promising clustering-based discriminant of PBH seeding models.

AI.tldr: This paper compares astrophysically-seeded black holes (light and heavy seeds) with primordial black holes (PBHs) to explain JWST's observations of over-massive black holes in metal-poor hosts at z~5-10. Only PBHs and Eddington-limited heavy seeding models can simultaneously match observed black hole masses, stellar masses, and extremely low metallicities. PBHs uniquely predict a decreasing black hole-to-stellar mass ratio with increasing halo mass, offering a clustering-based discriminant.
AI.motivation: JWST has revealed massive black holes in the early universe (z~5-10) that are over-massive relative to their host galaxies (obese black holes) and reside in metal-poor environments, challenging existing theoretical models for black hole formation at early epochs.
AI.method: The authors compare two models: (1) DELPHI semi-analytic model for astrophysically-seeded black holes (light ~100 M⊙ seeds and heavy ~10^3-5 M⊙ seeds) with growth via mergers and accretion (Eddington-limited and super-Eddington); (2) PHANES analytic model for cosmologically-seeded primordial black holes (PBHs, mass range 10^0.5-6 M⊙) growing only via sub-Eddington accretion.
AI.result: Eddington-limited accretion onto light seeds is ruled out. Both PBHs and heavy seeds with super-Eddington accretion can reproduce high Mbh/M* ratios (~0.3-1). Only PBHs and Eddington-limited heavy seeding models simultaneously reproduce Mbh, M*, and extremely low metallicities (Z ≤ 0.01 Z⊙). PBHs uniquely show decreasing Mbh/M* ratio with increasing halo mass at all redshifts, opposite to astrophysical models.
AI.conclusion: Systems at z~7 with Mbh/M* > 0.1, bolometric luminosities ~10^44-46 erg/s, residing in 10^9-11 M⊙ halos, showing negative Mbh/M* trend with halo mass, provide a promising clustering-based discriminant to identify PBH seeding models.

title: Energy Loss of Newborn Magnetars by Schwinger Process
comment: Comments are welcome!
summary: We investigate electron--positron pair creation through the Schwinger process in newborn magnetars with millisecond spin periods and surface dipole fields close to or above the QED critical field, $B_{\rm Q} = 4.414\times10^{13}\,\mathrm{G}$. In the unscreened field scenario, we derive the analytical global pair creation flux and recast it into a compact form with accurate analytic approximations. For a fiducial model with $B_{\rm p} = 10^{14}\,\mathrm{G}$ and $P_0 = 1\,\mathrm{ms}$, the Schwinger channel exceeds the classical Goldreich--Julian particle supply by many orders of magnitude and becomes the dominant source of charges at the earliest stage of the magnetar. The associated discharge removes about $90\%$ of the initial rotational energy within 30 ms, suppresses the gravitational-wave loss channel, and implies that the observable millisecond phase is extremely short in this unscreened scenario. The rapid energy release over such a short timescale may also provide a viable power source for astrophysical transients. Extending the same fiducial model to $10^4\,\mathrm{yr}$ gives spin periods of order seconds, linking newborn millisecond magnetars to the mature magnetar population.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates electron-positron pair creation via the Schwinger process in newborn magnetars with millisecond spin periods and ultra-strong magnetic fields (~10¹⁴ G). The Schwinger channel vastly dominates over the classical Goldreich-Julian charge supply, discharging ~90% of rotational energy within 30 ms, suppressing gravitational-wave emission, and linking newborn millisecond magnetars to the mature second-period magnetar population. This rapid energy release may also power astrophysical transients.
AI.motivation: To understand the role of the Schwinger process (QED pair creation) as a dominant charge source in newborn magnetars with surface dipole fields near or above the QED critical field, and to assess its impact on rotational energy loss, gravitational-wave emissions, and observable millisecond magnetar lifetimes.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of the global pair creation flux in the unscreened field scenario, recast into a compact form with accurate analytic approximations. A fiducial model with B_p = 10¹⁴ G and P_0 = 1 ms is used to quantify the Schwinger channel's contribution relative to the classical Goldreich-Julian particle supply.
AI.result: The Schwinger channel exceeds the Goldreich-Julian supply by many orders of magnitude, becoming the dominant charge source at the earliest magnetar stage. The discharge removes ~90% of initial rotational energy within 30 ms, suppressing gravitational-wave loss. Extrapolating to 10⁴ yr yields spin periods of order seconds, linking newborn to mature magnetars.
AI.conclusion: The Schwinger process dominates charge production in newborn millisecond magnetars with super-QED fields, causing extremely rapid spin-down (~30 ms) and suppressing gravitational-wave emission. This makes the observable millisecond phase very short and may power astrophysical transients. The model also naturally connects newborn magnetars to the observed mature magnetar population with second-scale spin periods.

title: Chaotic migration of LISA Extreme Mass Ratio Inspirals in a turbulent accretion disk: effect on waveform de-phasing
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to ApJL. Comments welcome
summary: Gravitational wave (GW) detector LISA will observe near-coalescence extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs), which typically form in galactic central accretion disks. Gas torques on EMRI will alter its GW-driven inspiral trajectory from the vacuum expectation, leading to potentially LISA-observable GW dephasing ($Δψ_{\rm gas}$). Most studies compute $Δψ_{\rm gas}$ for a thin, laminar disk, with negligible flow turbulence, where the disk exerts a fairly well-understood linear torque ($T_{\rm lin}$). However, these disks must be turbulent due to magneto-rotational instability in the inner regions. Hence, we present a proof-of-concept general, agnostic prescription for the turbulent torque ($T_{\rm turb}$) acting on an EMRI by modeling it as a Gaussian distribution around $T_{\rm lin}$, based on recent advances from a global hydrodynamical (HD) study. We compute $Δψ_{\rm gas}$ for the ``golden'' circular EMRI with total source mass $M=10^6~{\rm M}_\odot$ and mass ratio $q=5\times10^{-5}$ in its final four-year evolution at redshift $z=0.276$ and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) $=50$ by varying Eddington ratio ${\rm f}_{\rm Edd}$, turbulence normalization $C$ ($=~360$ in the aforementioned HD study), disk aspect ratio $h_0$, and turbo-viscous coefficient $α$ in a reasonable parameters space. We find that for ${\rm f}_{\rm Edd}\gtrsim0.3$, $C\gtrsim300$, $h_0\gtrsim0.03$, and $α\gtrsim0.1$, gas-induced dephasings are unobservable if only considering $T_{\rm lin}$ but could become detectable ($Δψ_{\rm gas}>8/$SNR) if EMRIs exhibit chaotic migration due to turbulent gas flow. Hence, this work motivates running MHD simulations of accretion disks with embedded LISA EMRIs in the early in-spiral phase over long enough timescales to understand the evolution of their orbital elements and the imprint of the turbulent environment on their gravitational waveforms.

AI.tldr: This paper models turbulent gas torques (as Gaussian distributions around linear torques) on LISA EMRI systems and shows that while linear torques alone produce unobservable dephasing for high Eddington ratios, turbulent migration can generate detectable GW dephasing (Δψ_gas > 8/SNR), motivating future MHD simulations.
AI.motivation: Previous studies computed GW dephasing for EMRIs embedded in thin, laminar disks with well-understood linear torques, but real accretion disks in galactic centers are turbulent due to magneto-rotational instability (MRI). The impact of this turbulence on LISA-observable dephasing was unknown.
AI.method: The authors develop a proof-of-concept prescription modeling turbulent torque (T_turb) as a Gaussian distribution around the linear torque (T_lin), based on results from a global hydrodynamical study. They compute GW dephasing (Δψ_gas) for a "golden" circular EMRI (M=10^6 M⊙, q=5×10^-5) over its final four-year evolution at z=0.276 (SNR=50), varying Eddington ratio f_Edd, turbulence normalization C, disk aspect ratio h_0, and viscosity parameter α.
AI.result: For f_Edd ≳ 0.3, C ≳ 300, h_0 ≳ 0.03, and α ≳ 0.1, linear torque-only dephasing is unobservable, but including turbulent torque can produce detectable dephasing (Δψ_gas > 8/SNR) due to chaotic EMRI migration.
AI.conclusion: Turbulent gas flows can make GW dephasing detectable where linear torques alone would not, motivating future MHD simulations of accretion disks with embedded LISA EMRIs over long timescales to better understand orbital evolution and turbulent imprints on gravitational waveforms.

title: Constraining Dark Matter Density Profiles in UFDs with Wide Binaries: Forecast for the Chinese Space Station Survey Telescope
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures, 1 table, accepted for publication on MNRAS
summary: The internal structure of dark matter halos on sub-galactic scales remains a key open question, particularly in the context of the core-cusp problem. Ultra-faint dwarf galaxies (UFDs), owing to their extreme dark matter dominance, provide a promising laboratory to probe these density profiles through stellar tracers. In this work, we assess the capability of the Chinese Space Station Telescope (CSST) to detect and characterize wide binary stars in the nearby UFD Segue 1, using mock observations. We generate mock binary populations based on our existing $N$-body simulations and incorporate realistic CSST observational conditions, including the expected deep-field limiting magnitude ($g \sim 27.5$ mag) and a photometric completeness of approximately $90\%$. The two-point correlation function (2PCF) of stellar pairs is used as a statistical tool to recover the binary fraction under these assumptions. We find that CSST can robustly detect wide binaries at the $3σ$ level for binary fractions as low as $f_b \gtrsim 0.01$, provided a stellar sample size of $N_{\mathrm{star}} \gtrsim 2300$. However, distinguishing between cusped and cored dark matter profiles is significantly more demanding, requiring $N_{\mathrm{star}} \gtrsim 6000$ and $f_b \gtrsim 0.1$ within $\sim 40\mathrm{kpc}$.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates the Chinese Space Station Telescope's (CSST) ability to detect wide binary stars in the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Segue 1 and distinguish between cusped and cored dark matter halo profiles using mock observations and two-point correlation function analysis.
AI.motivation: To address the core-cusp problem in dark matter halos on sub-galactic scales by using ultra-faint dwarf galaxies (UFDs) as dark matter-dominated laboratories to probe density profiles through stellar tracers.
AI.method: Mock binary populations were generated from N-body simulations, incorporating realistic CSST observational conditions (g ~ 27.5 mag limiting magnitude, ~90% photometric completeness). The two-point correlation function (2PCF) of stellar pairs was used to statistically recover binary fractions and distinguish dark matter profiles.
AI.result: CSST can detect wide binaries at 3σ significance for binary fractions fb ≳ 0.01 with Nstar ≳ 2300. Distinguishing cusped vs. cored profiles requires Nstar ≳ 6000 and fb ≳ 0.1 within ~40 kpc.
AI.conclusion: CSST is capable of detecting wide binaries in Segue 1, but differentiating dark matter halo profile types demands significantly larger stellar samples and higher binary fractions than detection alone.

title: Multi-wavelength study of EP250416a / GRB 250416C: An Optically Dark Long GRB with a Late Jet Break
comment: 12 pages, 14 figures, 4 tables, submitted to Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: We present multi-wavelength study of the $γ$/X-ray transient EP250416a (also designated GRB 250416C), triggered by the Einstein Probe (EP) Wide-field X-ray Telescope and also by SVOM and Konus-Wind. Observations spanning the gamma-ray, X-ray, and optical bands facilitated detailed analysis of the burst's prompt emission, afterglow evolution, and physical origin. EP250416a exhibits a burst duration of 30 s in X-ray and 17.7 s in gamma-rays, with joint spectral fitting of 0.5-5000 keV data gives $E\rm_{peak}=342_{-232}^{+90}$ keV. Optical spectroscopy of the afterglow, acquired with the Gemini Multi-Object Spectrograph (GMOS) on Gemini South, yielded a redshift of $z=0.963$. Accounting for the measured redshift, the isotropic energies are $E\rm_{X,iso}=2.7_{-0.5}^{+0.9}\times10^{50}$ erg and $E\rm_{γ,iso}=7.34_{-2.1}^{+5.1}\times10^{51}$ erg, aligning with the Amati relation for long GRBs. The fluence ratio $\rm S(25-50~keV)/S(50-100~keV)=0.78_{-0.15}^{+0.1}$ classifies EP250416a as an X-ray rich (XRR) GRB. The X-ray afterglow shows an initial shallow decay ($α\approx -0.5$) transitioning to a canonical decay phase ($α\approx -1$), with a very late jet break at $t\sim 1.5\times 10^6$ s, corresponding to a jet half-opening angle of $θ_j=10.6_{-1.8}^{+1.9}$ degrees. EP250416a is optically dark, as it shows only a faint $r$-band detection ($r=24.16$ mag) from Gemini South-GMOS and a low optical-to-X-ray spectral index $β_{\rm OX} = 0.3$. This may be attributed to significant host-galaxy extinction, with a required $A_V^{\text{host}}=5.5\ \text{mag}$ derived from the extinction curve model.

AI.tldr: EP250416a (GRB 250416C) is an X-ray rich, optically dark long GRB at z=0.963, with multi-wavelength analysis from EP, SVOM, Konus-Wind, and Gemini South, showing a jet break at ~1.5×10⁶ s, half-opening angle ~10.6°, and significant host extinction (A_V=5.5 mag).
AI.motivation: To characterize the multi-wavelength properties of the newly discovered γ/X-ray transient EP250416a (GRB 250416C) triggered by Einstein Probe, SVOM, and Konus-Wind, and to determine its physical origin via prompt emission, afterglow evolution, and broadband spectral analysis.
AI.method: Joint spectral fitting of 0.5–5000 keV data; optical spectroscopy with Gemini South/GMOS for redshift measurement; X-ray afterglow light-curve modeling; extinction curve modeling to derive host galaxy dust extinction (A_V).
AI.result: Duration: 30 s (X-ray), 17.7 s (γ-ray); E_peak = 342 keV; redshift z=0.963; isotropic energies E_X,iso=2.7×10⁵⁰ erg, E_γ,iso=7.34×10⁵¹ erg; fluence ratio S(25-50)/S(50-100)=0.78 → XRR GRB; afterglow decay α: -0.5 → -1; jet break at 1.5×10⁶ s, θ_j=10.6°; r-band magnitude 24.16, β_OX=0.3 (optically dark); host A_V=5.5 mag.
AI.conclusion: EP250416a is a long GRB consistent with the Amati relation; its optically dark nature is likely due to high host-galaxy extinction rather than an intrinsically faint afterglow; the jet break implies a moderately collimated outflow.

title: Orbital evolution of highly eccentric bodies embedded in a ringed accretion disc
comment: 16 pages, 19 figures. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Various processes can induce long-lived overdense rings and arcs in protoplanetary and AGN accretion discs, such as the accumulation of gas at the outer edge of the dead zone, or the infall of material. Using the local approximation of dynamical friction, we investigate the orbital evolution of a low-mass highly-eccentric point-mass accretor (perturber) embedded in an isothermal disc hosting a density ring. We specifically consider the regime in which the eccentricity exceeds four times the disc aspect ratio. For prograde perturbers, orbits that cross the ring progressively circularize while their semi-major axes converge toward the ring radius. As a result, perturbers accumulate, forming a population ring superimposed on the gaseous ring. The ring therefore acts as a migration trap for these eccentric orbits. We also find that prograde orbits tangent to the ring, either at apocentre or pericentre, remain tangential throughout their evolution; perturbers confined to these trajectories experience the highest accretion rates. In contrast, retrograde perturbers always migrate inward. Once the semi-major axis becomes smaller than the ring radius, the eccentricity grows, but not enough for the orbit to intersect the ring again. We also discuss how feedback effects, such as jet launching and thermal torques, could modify the effective forces acting on the perturbers.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how low-mass, highly-eccentric perturbers (like planets or stellar-mass objects) evolve orbitally in protoplanetary/AGN discs that contain a gas density ring. Using local dynamical friction approximations, the authors find that prograde orbits crossing the ring become trapped—their orbits circularize and semi-major axes converge to the ring radius, forming a population ring. Retrograde perturbers instead always migrate inward and cannot re-cross the ring.
AI.motivation: To understand how overdense rings/arcs in accretion discs (e.g., at the outer edge of dead zones or from infall) affect the orbital evolution and accumulation of low-mass, highly-eccentric point-mass perturbers, potentially explaining observed gaps, rings, and planet/object trapping.
AI.method: Local approximation of dynamical friction applied to a low-mass, highly-eccentric point-mass perturber embedded in an isothermal disc with a density ring. The regime studied is where eccentricity > 4× disc aspect ratio. Both prograde and retrograde orbits are considered analytically.
AI.result: Prograde perturbers crossing the ring circularize and converge toward the ring radius (migration trap). Orbits tangent to the ring remain tangential and experience highest accretion rates. Retrograde perturbers always migrate inward; once inside the ring, eccentricity grows but insufficiently to re-intersect the ring.
AI.conclusion: Density rings in accretion discs act as migration traps for highly-eccentric prograde perturbers, promoting their accumulation into a population ring. Feedback effects (jets, thermal torques) could further modify the effective forces and need future investigation.

title: Early metal-enriched baryon cycling before the midpoint of cosmic reionization
comment: To be resubmitted after revision; Nature Astronomy
summary: Models predict that chemical enrichment and gas redistribution should proceed rapidly once star formation begins, yet direct observational constraints at the earliest cosmic epochs have been scarce. Here we present evidence that metal-enriched gas in multiple ionic phases was already present around galaxies before the midpoint of cosmic reionization. Using JWST/NIRSpec rest-frame ultraviolet spectroscopy of three galaxies at redshifts $z=7.2-9.3$, we detect blueshifted metal absorption in all three systems; across the sample, the detected transitions span neutral, low-ionization, and high-ionization species, including O I, Si II, C II, Si IV, and C IV. These absorption features show velocity offsets of order $|Δv| \sim 50$--$250\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$, predominantly blueshifted relative to the systemic redshifts of the host galaxies derived from nebular emission lines. This ionic coexistence within a broadly shared velocity structure, together with the observed equivalent-width ratios, is consistent with outflowing or otherwise kinematically disturbed galaxy-associated gas, similar to that seen at lower redshift. The observations therefore indicate that metal-enriched gas associated with galaxies was already kinematically disturbed at very early times, requiring rapid metal production in the early generations of stars. These results show that key conditions for baryon cycling were established in at least a subset of luminous galaxies within the first several hundred million years of cosmic time, well before the completion of reionization.

AI.tldr: JWST/NIRSpec reveals metal-enriched, kinematically disturbed gas in multiple ionic phases around galaxies at redshifts z=7.2–9.3, demonstrating that chemical enrichment and baryon cycling were already underway within the first several hundred million years of cosmic history, before reionization ended.
AI.motivation: To provide direct observational constraints on chemical enrichment and gas redistribution in the earliest cosmic epochs, as models predict rapid enrichment post-star-formation but observational evidence has been scarce.
AI.method: JWST/NIRSpec rest-frame ultraviolet spectroscopy of three galaxies at z=7.2–9.3; detection of blueshifted metal absorption across neutral (O I), low-ionization (Si II, C II), and high-ionization (Si IV, C IV) species, with velocity offsets Δv ~ 50–250 km/s relative to systemic redshifts from nebular emission lines.
AI.result: All three galaxies show blueshifted metal absorption in multiple ionic phases coexisting within a shared velocity structure; equivalent-width ratios are consistent with outflowing or kinematically disturbed galaxy-associated gas analogous to lower-redshift systems.
AI.conclusion: Metal-enriched, kinematically disturbed gas existed around luminous galaxies before the midpoint of cosmic reionization, indicating rapid metal production and established baryon cycling within the first several hundred million years of cosmic time.

title: Gauging the Impact of Cosmic Ray Feedback on the Stellar Initial Mass Function
comment: 17 Pages, 9 Figures, published in ApJ. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2407.17597
summary: Cosmic rays (CRs) drive ionization and influence gas dynamics in molecular clouds (MCs), potentially impacting the resulting star formation outcomes. Although previous simulations of individual star formation have included methods for cosmic ray transport (CRT), none have been large enough to resolve the stellar initial mass function (IMF). We conduct numerical simulations following the collapse of a $20000 M_{\odot}$ MC and the subsequent star formation including CRT, both with and without CRs accelerated by winds from the young massive stars, and compare against a non-CRT simulation. We show that after the first massive stars form, the cavity produced by feedback is more pronounced in the CRT simulations because the external CRs are able to propagate inwards and compress the gas into higher density structures. This increases the subsequent star formation in the cloud; by the end of the simulation, the SFE in the CRT simulation including stellar wind CRs is 43 \% higher than the non-CRT simulation. The IMF is also top heavy in comparison, with a slope above 1 $M_{\odot}$ that is shallower by $\sim 20$ \%. These effects are also present in the simulation without wind-accelerated CRs, but they are not as pronounced; the SFE is only 16 \% higher than the non-CRT simulation, and the IMF high-mass slope is shallower by $\sim 10$ \%. These results may explain some of the observed top-heavy IMFs, which typically occur in high-CR environments such as the galactic center.

AI.tldr: Simulations of a 20,000 M☉ molecular cloud show that including cosmic ray transport (CRT) increases star formation efficiency (SFE) by up to 43% and produces a top-heavy IMF, especially when stellar wind-accelerated CRs are included, consistent with observations in high-CR environments like the galactic center.
AI.motivation: Previous star formation simulations included cosmic ray transport methods but were too small to resolve the stellar initial mass function (IMF); this study aims to bridge that gap by conducting larger simulations.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of a 20,000 M☉ molecular cloud collapse with CRT (including cases with and without wind-accelerated CRs from massive stars) compared against a non-CRT simulation.
AI.result: CRT simulations show more pronounced feedback cavities, higher SFE (43% higher with wind CRs, 16% higher without) and top-heavy IMFs (high-mass slope shallower by ~20% and ~10%, respectively) compared to non-CRT.
AI.conclusion: CRT significantly enhances star formation and produces top-heavy IMFs, potentially explaining observations in high-CR environments like the galactic center.

title: Supermassive Black Hole Winds in X-rays: SUBWAYS IV. Tracing Radio Emission and Unveiling the Role of Winds
comment: 25 pages, to appear in A&A
summary: Most Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) are Radio Quiet, with radio emission that may arise from star-formation activity, AGN-driven winds, weak jets, and coronal activity. Disentangling these mechanisms is challenging and requires detailed multi-wavelength investigation, but it is crucial for quantifying AGN feedback in galaxy evolution. We present a detailed radio investigation of 21 X-ray selected AGN in the Supermassive Black Hole Winds in X-Rays (SUBWAYS) sample (log Lbol = 44.9-46.3 erg/s, z=0.1-0.5), selected to systematically search for Ultra-Fast Outflows (UFOs). UFOs are detected in 30% of the targets, making the sample particularly well-suited for investigating the role and signatures of multi-scale outflows at different frequencies. We build the radio SED of the sources complementing our proprietary data, collected with the JVLA at 1.5 and 6 GHz, with images from LoTSS and other publicly available radio surveys between 150 and 1400 MHz. We investigate the role and occurrence of the aforementioned mechanisms, with particular interest in outflows and their possible relation with UFOs. We combined information on spectral indices, luminosities, and morphologies of the radio emission with properties derived in other wavebands, such as Star Formation Rate, X-ray luminosity, Eddington ratio or the UFO kinetic luminosity. All the sources are detected and are mostly consistent with RQ AGN. For 80% of the sources the data suggest the presence of an outflow (wind or weak jet). Interestingly, our results indicate that AGN with UFOs tend to have larger radio extension and a steep radio spectrum consistent with outflows. Moreover, the radio emission of the 6 UFO hosts is consistent with predictions from wind-driven shock models, possibly indicating a direct connection between the two phases. Alternatively, this may reflect physical conditions favouring the rise of both phenomena.

AI.tldr: Study of 21 X-ray selected AGN in SUBWAYS sample (z=0.1-0.5) reveals radio emission properties from winds/jets; 80% show outflow signatures, and UFO hosts exhibit larger radio extension and steep spectra consistent with wind-driven shock models, suggesting a connection between UFOs and radio outflows.
AI.motivation: To disentangle the mechanisms producing radio emission in Radio Quiet AGN (star formation, winds, weak jets, coronal activity) and quantify AGN feedback, by studying the connection between radio outflows and Ultra-Fast Outflows (UFOs).
AI.method: Analyzed radio SEDs of 21 X-ray selected AGN (log Lbol=44.9-46.3 erg/s, z=0.1-0.5) using proprietary JVLA data (1.5, 6 GHz) plus public surveys (LoTSS, others, 150-1400 MHz), combined with multi-wavelength properties (SFR, X-ray luminosity, Eddington ratio, UFO kinetic luminosity).
AI.result: All sources detected as RQ AGN; 80% show evidence of outflow (wind/weak jet). UFO hosts (30% of sample) tend to have larger radio extension and steep radio spectra consistent with outflows. Radio emission of UFO hosts matches predictions from wind-driven shock models.
AI.conclusion: A direct connection between UFOs and radio outflows is possible, or alternatively, physical conditions may favor the simultaneous occurrence of both phenomena.

title: Magnetar Engines in Broad-lined Type Ic Supernovae and a Unified Picture for Magnetar-powered Stripped-envelope Supernovae
comment: 39 pages, 17 figures, 3 tables, Comments are welcome!
summary: We model the multi-band lightcurves of 80 SNe Ic-BL, including 11 associated with lGRBs, using a magnetar engine model with $^{56}$Ni decay. We find that the data are all consistent with a magnetar central engine, and such a model yields high-quality fits across the sample. The medians with $1σ$ regions of the key parameters are $P_{\rm{i}}\sim2.04^{+1.84}_{-0.96}\,{\rm{ms}}$, $B_{\rm{p}}\sim3.96^{+3.28}_{-1.40}\times10^{15}\,{\rm{G}}$, $M_{\rm{ej}}\sim2.30^{+1.48}_{-1.02}\,M_\odot$, and $M_{\rm{Ni}}\sim0.18^{+0.14}_{-0.09}\,M_\odot$, with strong and statistically significant correlations observed for both $M_{\rm{ej}}-P_{\rm{i}}$ (anti-correlation) and $M_{\rm{Ni}}-M_{\rm{ej}}$ (correlation). Comparing the SN Ic-BL samples with and without lGRB association using fitting parameters, we find no significant difference between them, although the GRB-associated sample is slightly brighter, possibly due to an observational bias. Relative to ordinary SNe Ic, SNe Ic-BL have similar $^{56}$Ni and ejecta masses, suggesting comparable pre-SN progenitor properties, with differences possibly arising from the presence of a magnetar engine. In comparison with other possible magnetar-powered SESNe, including SLSNe Ic and FBOTs, we confirm a strong universal $M_{\rm{ej}}-P_{\rm{i}}$ correlation, indicating a common origin. SNe Ic-BL and SLSNe Ic have similar ejecta mass distributions, typically $M_{\rm ej}\gtrsim0.5\,M_\odot$, while FBOTs mostly lie below this value. Differences between SNe Ic-BL and SLSNe Ic may arise from magnetar properties, with SN Ic-BL magnetars rotating faster and having stronger fields. Moreover, the $P_{\rm{i}}-B_{\rm{p}}$ distribution of lGRB magnetars largely overlaps with that of SN Ic-BL magnetars. In connection with binary simulation results, we propose a unified physical classification and progenitor framework for magnetar-powered and ordinary SESNe.

AI.tldr: 80 SNe Ic-BL (11 with lGRBs) are modeled with magnetar + ⁵⁶Ni decay. Median parameters: Pi~2.04 ms, Bp~3.96×10¹⁵ G, Mej~2.30 M☉, MNi~0.18 M☉. Significant Mej-Pi anti-correlation and MNi-Mej correlation found. No major difference between lGRB-associated and non-lGRB SNe Ic-BL. Compared with SLSNe Ic and FBOTs, a universal Mej-Pi correlation emerges. A unified classification framework for magnetar-powered SESNe is proposed.
AI.motivation: To systematically investigate the central engine properties of broad-lined Type Ic supernovae (SNe Ic-BL), both with and without long gamma-ray burst (lGRB) association, using a magnetar engine model, and to establish connections among different classes of stripped-envelope supernovae (SESNe).
AI.method: Multi-band lightcurve fitting of 80 SNe Ic-BL with a magnetar + ⁵⁶Ni heating model, extracting key parameters (spin period Pi, dipole magnetic field Bp, ejecta mass Mej, nickel mass MNi). Statistical correlation analysis and comparative studies across subclasses.
AI.result: All 80 SNe Ic-BL are consistent with a magnetar engine. Key parameters: Pi~2.04⁺¹.⁸⁴₋₀.₉₆ ms, Bp~3.96⁺³.²⁸₋₁.₄₀×10¹⁵ G, Mej~2.30⁺¹.⁴⁸₋₁.₀₂ M☉, MNi~0.18⁺⁰.¹⁴₋₀.₀₉ M☉. Strong Mej-Pi anti-correlation and MNi-Mej correlation. lGRB-associated sample slightly brighter but no significant parameter differences. SNe Ic-BL and SLSNe Ic share similar Mej distributions (≳0.5 M☉), while FBOTs have lower Mej. SN Ic-BL magnetars rotate faster and have stronger fields than SLSNe Ic magnetars.
AI.conclusion: SNe Ic-BL are consistently powered by magnetar engines. The presence or absence of an lGRB does not correspond to distinct magnetar parameter regimes. A universal Mej-Pi correlation across SESNe suggests a common progenitor origin. A unified physical classification framework for magnetar-powered and ordinary stripped-envelope supernovae is proposed based on magnetar parameters and binary evolution simulations.

title: SFUMATO#: a GPU accelerated code for Self-Gravitational Radiation Hydrodynamics Simulation with Adaptive Mesh Refinement
comment: 23 pages, 23 figures, accepted for publication in PASJ
summary: We present a new implementation of the SFUMATO code, called SFUMATO#, for solving self-gravitational radiation hydrodynamics problems using adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) with the CUDA/HIP programming frameworks. The code incorporates a multigrid solver for self-gravity, radiation transfer with M1 closure and reduced speed of light approximation, non-equilibrium chemistry, thermal evolution, and sink particle schemes. We develop new non-equilibrium chemistry and thermal solvers based on a linearized implicit method, whose accuracy is validated through a series of test problems by comparison with solutions obtained using the Newton-Raphson method. By incorporating the heat capacity of dust grains, the dust temperature can be evolved without iterative energy-balance calculations. From the perspective of computational cost, we demonstrate that adopting an increased pseudo dust heat capacity accelerates the chemistry solver while preserving accuracy, even when the value is increased by up to three orders of magnitude relative to the realistic value. In addition, we perform a suite of test problems to confirm the validity of the other components of our implementation. The code supports multi-GPU execution via MPI-based parallelization. We measure the strong-scaling performance of the hydrodynamics and self-gravity solvers on both uniform and AMR grids, as well as the overall code performance using a giant molecular cloud simulation. We find that the computational cost of the self-gravity solver increases with the number of MPI processes, indicating that efficient parallel performance is achieved only when the number of devices is chosen such that the cost of the self-gravity solver remains comparable to that of the other components.

AI.tldr: SFUMATO# is a GPU-accelerated radiation hydrodynamics code with AMR, featuring multigrid self-gravity, M1-closure radiation transfer, non-equilibrium chemistry, thermal evolution, and sink particles. It uses a linearized implicit chemistry solver, avoids iterative dust temperature calculations via pseudo heat capacity, and supports multi-GPU MPI parallelization. Strong scaling is effective only when the self-gravity solver cost is balanced with other components.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient GPU-accelerated implementation of the SFUMATO code for solving self-gravitational radiation hydrodynamics on AMR grids, addressing computational bottlenecks in chemistry, thermal evolution, and parallel scaling.
AI.method: Implemented SFUMATO# with CUDA/HIP, multigrid Poisson solver, M1-closure RT with reduced speed of light, linearized implicit non-equilibrium chemistry/thermal solver, dust temperature via pseudo heat capacity, sink particles, and MPI-based multi-GPU parallelization.
AI.result: The linearized implicit solver matches Newton-Raphson accuracy. Pseudo dust heat capacity can be increased by 3 orders of magnitude without loss of accuracy, accelerating chemistry. Self-gravity solver cost grows with MPI processes, limiting scaling unless costs are balanced.
AI.conclusion: SFUMATO# is a validated, multi-GPU capable radiation hydrodynamics code with efficient chemistry/thermal solvers, but parallel efficiency of the self-gravity solver requires careful selection of device count to maintain cost balance.

title: An RXTE Search for the Sterile Neutrino Decay in Galaxy Clusters
summary: We have searched for the 3.55 keV line from sterile neutrino decay using 3.1 megaseconds of RXTE cluster data. A 2.5$σ$ excess of emission over a thermal model is found over the energy span of the 3.55 keV line in the combined spectra of the eight clusters that individually have an excess. The residuals are added to increase the signal to noise ratio of the excess, which is then modeled with a Gaussian to simulate the instrumental spectral response. We find a significant correlation (r = 0.76) for a line centered at 3.6 keV with a model flux of 3.07 x 10$^{-5}$ ph cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$. Mixing angle for detected clusters ranges from 0.35 to 6.2 x 10$^{-10}$. The decay rate inferred from the line flux is strongly correlated (r = 0.87) with cluster temperature, which is due to hotter, more massive clusters having a larger amount of dark matter. Approximately half of the decay line total flux comes from the Coma cluster. We fit the Coma cluster spectrum with two different three-component models. The first includes a Gaussian fixed at 3.55 keV to model soft emission. The second three-component model uses a second thermal component to model soft emission. The model fit parameters indicate that the second thermal component is modeling high-energy residuals rather than low ones, where the Gaussian is. Though our line fluxes exceed most reported detections and upper limits, they do not overproduce the dark matter. We conclude that some fraction of the marginally detected excess could be attributed to the decay line since low-temperature thermal emission and systematics fail to model it completely.

AI.tldr: Using 3.1 Ms of RXTE cluster data, a 2.5σ excess at ~3.55 keV is found in eight clusters, correlating with cluster temperature (r=0.87) and dark matter content. The Coma cluster contributes half the flux. Systematics and thermal models cannot fully account for the excess, leaving room for a sterile neutrino decay interpretation.
AI.motivation: To search for the 3.55 keV X-ray line predicted from sterile neutrino dark matter decay, using a large archival RXTE dataset to test for a signal that could indicate beyond-Standard-Model physics in galaxy clusters.
AI.method: Analyzed 3.1 Ms of RXTE spectra from multiple clusters; stacked residuals from eight clusters with individual excesses; modeled the combined excess with a Gaussian; tested correlation of line flux with cluster temperature; fit the Coma cluster with two three-component models (one with a fixed 3.55 keV Gaussian, the other with a second thermal component).
AI.result: A 2.5σ excess at ~3.6 keV; model flux of 3.07×10⁻⁵ ph cm⁻² s⁻¹; mixing angles of 0.35–6.2×10⁻¹⁰; strong correlation (r=0.87) between decay rate and cluster temperature; Coma cluster contributes ~50% of total flux; the second thermal component models high-energy residuals, not the soft excess where the Gaussian is.
AI.conclusion: A fraction of the marginally detected excess could be attributable to sterile neutrino decay, as low-temperature thermal emission and systematics cannot fully model it, though the line fluxes exceed most previous detections and upper limits.

title: Signatures of Very Massive Stars in the Epoch of Reionization
comment: Submitted to A&A Letters, 4 pages and 2 Figures (+Appendix)
summary: We present ultra-deep ($\simeq 20-30$ hours), rest-frame UV spectroscopy with NIRSpec/JWST of two UV-bright galaxies at $z\sim 8.7$, CEERS-1019 and CEERS-1025 ($Z_{\rm neb} \simeq 0.1 Z_{\odot}$). The spectra reveal exceptionally strong P-Cygni profiles in wind lines (NV $λ$1240 and CIV $λ$1550) and significant broad and strong HeII $λ$1640 emission ($\rm EW\simeq 2-4$ A). We compare the observations with synthetic stellar population models at $Z_{\star} \simeq 0.1 Z_{\odot}$, both including and excluding very massive stars (VMS). Models including VMS provide a markedly improved fit to the data relative to non-VMS models ($Δ$AIC and $Δ$BIC $> 70$), which fail to reproduce the observed strengths of the wind features. A comparison with empirical spectra of VMS-dominated systems in the local Universe further supports this interpretation. The best-fit VMS models imply extremely young ages of the stellar populations ($\simeq 1.5-2.0$Myr) and high ionizing photon production efficiencies ($\log ξ_{\rm ion} [\rm Hz erg^{-1}] \gtrsim 25.8$), exceeding those inferred from non-VMS models by $\sim 0.1-0.2$ dex. These results provide evidence for an overabundance of VMS at high-$z$ with an IMF extending well beyond $100 M_{\odot}$, and highlight their potential role in shaping the rest-frame UV spectra, chemical enrichment, and ionizing output of galaxies in the early Universe.

AI.tldr: JWST/NIRSpec ultra-deep spectroscopy of two z~8.7 galaxies reveals strong P-Cygni profiles and HeII emission consistent with very massive stars (VMS). Models including VMS fit the data significantly better than standard models, implying top-heavy IMFs extending beyond 100 Msun, young ages (~1.5-2 Myr), and high ionizing photon production efficiencies (log ξion ≳ 25.8).
AI.motivation: To investigate whether very massive stars (VMS) are present in high-redshift galaxies and how they influence the observed UV spectra, ionizing output, and chemical enrichment in the early Universe.
AI.method: Ultra-deep (~20-30 hr) rest-frame UV NIRSpec/JWST spectroscopy of two UV-bright galaxies at z~8.7 (CEERS-1019, CEERS-1025) with Zneb~0.1 Z⊙, compared to synthetic stellar population models (with and without VMS) at Z⋆~0.1 Z⊙, and empirical spectra of local VMS-dominated systems.
AI.result: VMS models provide a markedly improved fit (ΔAIC and ΔBIC > 70). Non-VMS models fail to reproduce observed wind features (NV λ1240, CIV λ1550 P-Cygni profiles, broad HeII λ1640 with EW~2-4 Å). Best-fit VMS models imply ages ~1.5-2 Myr and log ξion ≳ 25.8 Hz erg⁻¹, ~0.1-0.2 dex higher than non-VMS models.
AI.conclusion: The observations provide evidence for an overabundance of VMS at high redshift with an IMF extending beyond 100 M⊙, highlighting their role in shaping rest-frame UV spectra, chemical enrichment, and ionizing output of early galaxies.

title: Mitigating Systematic Errors in Parameter Estimation of Binary Black Hole Mergers in O1-O3 LIGO-Virgo Data
comment: 23 pages, 13 figures, 2 tables. Comments are welcome
summary: Systematic errors in the parameter estimation (PE) of gravitational wave (GW) mergers can arise from various sources, including waveform systematics, noise mischaracterization, data analysis artifacts, and other unknown factors. In this study, we analyze selected events from the first three observing runs of the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) collaboration. We choose events that have been flagged in various studies as potentially affected by systematic errors. Here, we reanalyze these events using a couple of parametric models developed in previous work that incorporate uncertainties in both the phase and amplitude of the GW waveform. In this data-driven approach, we apply sufficiently broad priors on the uncertainty parameters to account for potential systematic errors. Our findings show that the proposed method effectively reduces systematic errors, even those arising from data artifacts, such as glitches occurring near a signal and the deglitching process in GW frame files. Similarly, inconsistent results from different waveform models become much more consistent in our framework. One noteworthy event we examine is GW191109\_010717, which is particularly interesting due to its anti-aligned spin properties. We report that, within our framework, the event still exhibits anti-aligned spin characteristics, but the inference results become consistent across raw and deglitched frame files, as well as across the waveform models used for this event (IMRPhenomXPHM, IMRPhenomXO4a, and NRSur7dq4). A similar trend is observed for the event GW200129\_065458, which previously yielded a high, but inconsistent precession parameter among different waveform models. In contrast, we observe a non-zero and consistent value of $χ_{p}=0.60^{+0.31}_{-0.33}, 0.58^{+0.30}_{-0.29}$ and $0.56^{+0.31}_{-0.28}$ for the IMRPhenomXPHM, IMRPhenomXO4a, and NRSur7dq4 waveform models, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes GW events from LVK's first three observing runs, applying parametric models with broad priors on uncertainty parameters to correct systematic errors in parameter estimation. The method reduces inconsistencies across waveform models and handles artifacts like glitches, showing consistent results for events like GW191109_010717 and GW200129_065458.
AI.motivation: Systematic errors in gravitational wave parameter estimation can arise from waveform modeling inaccuracies, noise mischaracterization, data glitches, and other artifacts, potentially leading to biased or inconsistent inferred source properties across different analysis methods.
AI.method: A data-driven approach using parametric models with broad priors on uncertainty parameters that incorporate both phase and amplitude uncertainties in GW waveforms, applied to reanalyze events flagged for potential systematics from LVK O1-O3 runs.
AI.result: The method effectively reduces systematic errors from glitches, deglitching processes, and waveform model inconsistencies. GW191109_010717 retains anti-aligned spin properties but becomes consistent across raw/deglitched data and multiple waveform models. GW200129_065458 yields consistent precession parameters across IMRPhenomXPHM (χ_p=0.60), IMRPhenomXO4a (χ_p=0.58), and NRSur7dq4 (χ_p=0.56).
AI.conclusion: The proposed parametric uncertainty framework successfully mitigates systematic errors in GW parameter estimation, yielding consistent results across different waveform models and data processing conditions for previously problematic events.

title: SPURS: Bursty Star Formation in an Extremely Luminous Weak Emission Line Galaxy at $z=9.3$
comment: 35 pages, 19 figures, 3 tables. Comments welcome!
summary: JWST has revealed a population of super-luminous early galaxies with a volume density in excess of most expectations. The spectra reveal diverse properties: while some reveal strong emission lines characteristic of galaxies in the midst of strong bursts, others show weak emission lines that could reflect old stellar populations, large escape fractions, or post-burst star formation histories. Through the JWST Cycle 4 large program SPURS, we have obtained ultra-deep (29 hr) rest-frame UV spectroscopy of a z=9.3 super-luminous ($M_{\rm UV}=-21.66$) galaxy with large assembled stellar mass (1.6$\times$10$^9$ $M_\odot$) and extremely weak emission lines (H$β$ EW $\approx25$~Å). The strong stellar wind features and rest-optical line ratios suggest the galaxy is already significantly enriched, with a metallicity of 0.4--0.7~Z$_\odot$. The interstellar absorption lines reveal outflows ($v\simeq -161$~km~s$^{-1}$) with a large neutral gas covering fraction, suggesting that the weak emission lines are not due to large escape fractions. The combination of the Balmer break, weak emission lines, and stellar wind features constrains the star formation history, indicating a recent burst of star formation lasting 10--20 Myr followed by a downturn over the last 10~Myr. The observations suggest that $z\gtrsim 9$ weak emission line galaxies such as this source can be explained by stochastic star formation, provided that the downturns in star formation are recent (i.e., <10 Myr prior to observation). The ultra-deep grating spectrum enables the IGM damping wing to be characterized, decoupling the effects of local absorption. The smooth Ly$α$ break indicates that this source, one of the most massive galaxies known at z>9, is likely situated in a small ionized bubble ($0.29_{-0.09}^{+0.11}$~pMpc), as is common at large neutral hydrogen fractions ($\bar{x}_{\rm HI}=0.81_{-0.21}^{+0.14}$).

AI.tldr: JWST's SPURS program studied a super-luminous galaxy at z=9.3 with weak emission lines, finding it has high stellar mass (1.6×10⁹ M☉), significant metallicity (0.4-0.7 Z☉), outflowing gas, and a recent burst followed by a downturn in star formation. It resides in a small ionized bubble (0.29 pMpc) within a largely neutral IGM (x_HI=0.81).
AI.motivation: To understand the nature of super-luminous early galaxies discovered by JWST that have weak emission lines, which could indicate old stellar populations, large escape fractions, or post-burst star formation histories—phenomena that challenge standard models of early galaxy evolution.
AI.method: Ultra-deep (29 hr) rest-frame UV spectroscopy from JWST Cycle 4 SPURS program targeting a z=9.3 super-luminous galaxy, analyzing stellar wind features, rest-optical line ratios, interstellar absorption lines, Balmer break, and IGM damping wing profile.
AI.result: The galaxy is significantly enriched (0.4-0.7 Z☉), shows outflows (~-161 km/s) with high neutral gas covering fraction (ruling out large escape fractions as cause of weak lines), and experienced a 10-20 Myr burst followed by a downturn in the last 10 Myr. The Lyα break indicates a small ionized bubble (0.29 pMpc) in a mostly neutral IGM (x_HI=0.81).
AI.conclusion: Weak emission line galaxies at z≳9 can be explained by stochastic star formation with recent downturns (<10 Myr prior to observation). Such massive galaxies at high redshift are commonly situated in small ionized bubbles within a largely neutral IGM.

title: Testing solitonic boson star interpretations of Sagittarius A* with near-infrared flare astrometry
summary: We use GRAVITY near-infrared (NIR) flare astrometry to test whether Sagittarius A* could be a solitonic boson star. We consider five spherically symmetric solitonic boson-star models with different effective radii, together with the Schwarzschild black hole. Treating the flares as hot spots on circular equatorial orbits, we analyze their centroid motions and images in these spacetimes and use them for parameter fitting. We perform the fitting using both $χ^2$ analysis and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, which yield consistent results: the inferred masses of boson-star models are systematically larger than the established value of $4.3\times10^6M_\odot$. Notably, more diffusive boson stars exhibit imaging properties closer to those of a black hole, leading to mass estimates that are correspondingly closer to the established value. Overall, our results place stringent constraints on solitonic boson star interpretations of Sagittarius A*, although do not completely rule them out.

AI.tldr: This paper tests whether Sagittarius A* could be a solitonic boson star by fitting GRAVITY NIR flare astrometry data with five boson-star models and a Schwarzschild black hole model, using χ² and MCMC methods. Results show boson-star models yield systematically higher mass estimates than the established 4.3×10⁶ M⊙, with more diffusive boson stars approaching black-hole-like imaging properties. The study places stringent constraints on but does not completely rule out boson star interpretations.
AI.motivation: To test the hypothesis that Sagittarius A* (the supermassive compact object at the Galactic Center) could be a solitonic boson star rather than a black hole, using observational astrometric data of near-infrared flares.
AI.method: Spherically symmetric solitonic boson-star models (five with different effective radii) and a Schwarzschild black hole model are considered. Flares are treated as hot spots on circular equatorial orbits; centroid motions and images are analyzed. Parameter fitting is performed using both χ² analysis and Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.
AI.result: Inferred masses of boson-star models are systematically larger than the established value of 4.3×10⁶ M⊙. More diffusive boson stars exhibit imaging properties closer to a black hole, yielding mass estimates closer to the established value.
AI.conclusion: Stringent constraints are placed on solitonic boson star interpretations of Sagittarius A*, but the possibility is not completely ruled out.

title: An Old, Low-mass, Metal-poor Hypervelocity Star Candidate Consistent with a Galactic Center Origin
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, Accepted for publication in ApJL
summary: We report the discovery of DESI-HVS1, a hypervelocity star (HVS) candidate identified from DESI DR1 spectroscopy and Gaia DR3 astrometry. DESI-HVS1 is an old, low-mass, metal-poor F-type star with a mass of $0.8\,M_\odot$, an age of $\sim14.1$~Gyr, and $\mathrm{[Fe/H]}=-1.6$. It is located at a heliocentric distance of $3.77^{+0.39}_{-0.36}$~kpc and has a Galactocentric total velocity of $523^{+46}_{-47}\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$, marginally exceeding the local escape speed, corresponding to an unbound probability of $P_{\rm ub} \sim 50\%$. Backward orbit integrations show that DESI-HVS1 had a closest approach to the Galactic Centre (GC) of $0.40^{+0.23}_{-0.11}\,\mathrm{kpc}$, with a velocity of $682^{+22}_{-35}\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$ and a flight time of $12.89^{+0.92}_{-0.74}\,\mathrm{Myr}$. The reconstructed orbit exhibits a clear perigalactic turning point and only a single crossing of the Galactic midplane ($P_{\rm cross} > 0.95$). These properties suggest that DESI-HVS1 is most naturally explained by the Hills mechanism, although alternative scenarios cannot be entirely ruled out. Its discovery provides the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS candidate consistent with a GC origin, indicating that the apparent dominance of young, massive GC-origin HVSs is likely a consequence of observational selection effects.

AI.tldr: DESI-HVS1 is a hypervelocity star (HVS) candidate discovered from DESI DR1 spectroscopy and Gaia DR3 astrometry. It is an old (14.1 Gyr), low-mass (0.8 M☉), metal-poor ([Fe/H]=-1.6) F-type star at ~3.77 kpc with a Galactocentric velocity of 523 km/s (~50% unbound probability). Orbit integrations show a close approach to the Galactic Center (0.40 kpc) with a flight time of ~12.9 Myr, consistent with the Hills mechanism. This is the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS from the GC, suggesting observational bias explains the dominance of young, massive HVSs in prior samples.
AI.motivation: To discover and characterize a hypervelocity star candidate that may originate from the Galactic Center, and to test whether the observed dominance of young, massive HVSs is due to observational selection effects rather than intrinsic properties of HVS populations.
AI.method: Identification using DESI DR1 spectroscopy and Gaia DR3 astrometry; analysis of stellar parameters (mass, age, metallicity) via spectroscopy; calculation of 3D velocities and escape speed; backward orbit integration to trace origin, closest approach to Galactic Center, flight time, and Galactic plane crossings.
AI.result: DESI-HVS1 is an old (~14.1 Gyr), low-mass (0.8 M☉), metal-poor ([Fe/H]=-1.6) F-type star at ~3.77 kpc with Galactocentric velocity 523 km/s (~50% unbound). It approached the GC within 0.40 kpc at 682 km/s, flight time 12.89 Myr, with a single Galactic midplane crossing (P_cross > 0.95), consistent with the Hills mechanism.
AI.conclusion: The discovery provides the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS candidate consistent with a GC origin, indicating that the apparent dominance of young, massive GC-origin HVSs is likely a consequence of observational selection effects.

title: Massive star formation at the Galactic crossroads: Insights from G358.69+0.03 in the Galactic center
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A. 14+11 pages, 18 figures
summary: We investigated the high-mass star formation activity in a subregion of the Sagittarius E star-forming complex, centered at (l,b) = (358.69 deg, 0.03 deg), where infrared and radio sources trace a prominent U-shaped structure that has not been identified in previous studies. We used radio continuum data from the Global View on Star Formation (GLOSTAR) survey, which is a wide-band radio (4-8 GHz) survey of the Milky Way that combines data from the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array and the Effelsberg 100 m telescope. Using BLOBCAT source extraction software, we identified 49 compact radio sources. Based on multiwavelength associations and spectral index estimates, we identified GLOSTAR counterparts to 27 previously confirmed HII regions, detected radio emission from 3 WISE "radio-quiet" candidates, and report 5 new HII region candidates. The derived physical properties indicate that most are relatively evolved HII regions. We find around 50 cold dust clumps, predominantly toward the south and southeast. Mid-infrared flux-ratio maps ([4.5]/[3.6]) show localized shock enhancements along the arc and adjacent clumps, and 15 clumps exhibit SiO emission with broad components indicative of shocks. Together with CO data, the SiO velocity components delineate a continuous (>100 km/s) velocity bridge that links the far dust-lane inflow to the central molecular zone (CMZ) stream. The largest concentration of clumps and compact HII regions lies at this interface. These combined diagnostics favor a scenario in which bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface compressed the gas and triggered the observed high-mass star formation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates high-mass star formation in a U-shaped subregion of the Sagittarius E complex using GLOSTAR radio continuum data, identifying 49 compact radio sources, confirming/identifying 35 HII regions, finding ~50 cold dust clumps with shock signatures, and proposing bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface as the triggering mechanism.
AI.motivation: To characterize the high-mass star formation activity in a previously unidentified U-shaped structure within the Sagittarius E star-forming complex, and to understand the dynamical processes triggering star formation in this region.
AI.method: Used GLOSTAR survey (4-8 GHz) radio continuum data from VLA and Effelsberg; applied BLOBCAT for source extraction; performed multiwavelength associations, spectral index estimates, and mid-infrared flux-ratio mapping; analyzed CO and SiO line data for kinematic signatures.
AI.result: Identified 49 compact radio sources, including 27 confirmed HII regions, 3 WISE radio-quiet candidates, and 5 new HII region candidates; found ~50 cold dust clumps with shock signatures; detected a continuous velocity bridge (>100 km/s) in SiO linking the far dust-lane to the CMZ stream.
AI.conclusion: Bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface compressed the gas and triggered the observed high-mass star formation activity.
